title: Case 01: The Spinning World
subtitle: Acute Vestibular Neuritis (Right Side)
coverDescription:
Target Learners: PGY Residents / Medical Students

Educational Goal: Differentiate peripheral from central vertigo (HINTS exam), interpret Caloric canal paresis, and understand VEMP in localizing vestibular nerve involvement.

preTest.title: Pre-Test (Baseline Assessment)
preTest.instructions: Before we begin the case, answer the following question to assess your baseline knowledge.

questions.text: A patient presents with acute onset continuous vertigo, nausea, and spontaneous left-beating nystagmus. Which finding would most strongly suggest a peripheral etiology?

options.text: A corrective "catch-up" saccade during the Head Impulse Test (HIT) to the right.
Skew deviation (vertical misalignment of the eyes).
Nystagmus that changes direction with gaze.

slides.content: A 42-year-old female presents to the ER with sudden, severe, constant vertigo that started 12 hours ago. She has intense nausea and has vomited twice. She denies any hearing loss, tinnitus, or focal neurological symptoms (no weakness, no numbness), she had URI symptoms in recent week.

slides.title: Problem List & History Taking
slides.instruction: The user must identify the "Essential Negative Findings" to rule out a stroke:

questionsAndRationales.question: 1. "Do you have any difficulty speaking or swallowing?"
2. "Did the vertigo start suddenly or gradually?"
questionsAndRationales.rationale: → Acute onset is typical for VN

questionsAndRationales.question: 3. "Any recent viral infections?"
questionsAndRationales.rationale: → VN is often preceded by an upper respiratory infection.

videoPlaceholder.label: HINTS Exam Demonstration Video
videoPlaceholder.note: Video placeholder — to be replaced with: Nystagmus, Head Impulse Test, Skew Test

steps.name: Nystagmus Observation
steps.description: The patient has spontaneous horizontal-torsional nystagmus beating to the LEFT.
steps.question: Does the nystagmus intensity increase or decrease when looking to the left?

steps.name: Head Impulse Test (HIT)
steps.description: Perform a virtual rapid head turn to the right.
steps.visual: The eyes move with the head and then perform a refixation saccade back to the center.

clinicalDecision.question: Is this a "Dangerous" or "Benign" HINTS profile?
clinicalDecision.reasoning: → If negative, this indicates a peripheral origin.

investigations.name: PTA (Pure Tone Audiometry)
investigations.question: Does the patient have hearing loss?
investigations.answer: No, hearing is within normal limits. This rules out Labyrinthitis.

investigations.name: Caloric Test
investigations.observation: Right-side irrigation shows significantly reduced response.
investigations.result: Right CP > 25% indicates right-side vestibular hypofunction.

investigations.name: VEMP (cVEMP / oVEMP)
rules: 🔴 Red Line: Indicates the Right Ear.
🔵 Blue Line: Indicates the Left Ear.

options.feedback: → Correct! Steroids may improve nerve recovery.
→ Incorrect. Bed rest hinders central compensation.
→ Correct! Early movement promotes brain adaptation.

interactive.instructions: Use the case data to explain your clinical reasoning. Focus on peripheral vs central clues.

questions.text: Which HINTS findings in this case support a peripheral vestibular lesion?
questions.hint: Think about HIT, skew deviation, and nystagmus direction.

feedbackTemplate.excellent: Good reasoning. You connected key HINTS clues such as {keyword} with a peripheral vestibular pattern.
feedbackTemplate.good: Good start. You identified {keyword}; add the other HINTS components to make the argument stronger.
feedbackTemplate.hint: Review the HINTS findings: abnormal HIT, no skew deviation, and unidirectional horizontal-torsional nystagmus support a peripheral lesion.

questions.text: Why does normal hearing make vestibular neuritis more likely than labyrinthitis?
questions.hint: Compare vestibular-only symptoms with cochlear involvement.

feedbackTemplate.excellent: Exactly. Mentioning {keyword} shows you understand that labyrinthitis usually includes cochlear involvement.
feedbackTemplate.good: Good direction with {keyword}. Make the contrast explicit: neuritis affects vestibular function without hearing loss.
feedbackTemplate.hint: Vestibular neuritis causes vertigo without hearing loss; labyrinthitis usually includes hearing symptoms because the cochlea is involved.

postTest.instructions: Answer these questions after completing the case. They revisit the most important learning points.

questions.text: In acute vestibular syndrome, which HINTS pattern is most consistent with a peripheral lesion?

options.text: Normal head impulse test, direction-changing nystagmus, and skew deviation.
Abnormal head impulse test, unidirectional nystagmus, and no skew deviation.
New focal weakness with normal vestibular testing.

questions.explanation: A peripheral HINTS pattern includes an abnormal head impulse test, unidirectional nystagmus, and absent skew deviation.

questions.text: Which finding would shift the diagnosis away from vestibular neuritis and toward labyrinthitis?

options.text: Right canal paresis on caloric testing.

questions.explanation: Labyrinthitis involves the inner ear labyrinth and commonly includes cochlear symptoms such as hearing loss or tinnitus.

learningPoints: Use HINTS to separate peripheral vestibular neuritis from central causes in acute vestibular syndrome.
Normal hearing supports vestibular neuritis over labyrinthitis.
Caloric weakness and VEMP findings help localize vestibular nerve involvement.
